qnaData.stem:
A 60 year old person presented with sudden onset of ocular pain and redness in left eye. There was history of cataract surgery in right eye. On examination Visual acuity in left eye was perception of light with accurate projection of rays. Anterior chamber was deep with fluffy material. Intraocular pressure in left eye was 50 mm of Hg. Right eye examination was essentially normal.

a) What is initial management?
b) What is the definitive management?
c) Classify lens induced glaucoma
d) Difference between Phacolytic and Phacomorphic glaucoma (2x4=8)

A 45 year old female presented with watering, redness and inability to open right eye from last 5 days. There was history of trauma with cow's tail in right eye. On examination visual acuity in right eye was finger counting close to face with whitish discoloration in cornea?

a) What is most probable diagnosis?
b) How will you diagnose this condition?
c) Agents used for management
d) Indications for surgical management (2+2+2+1=7)

qnaData.stem:
A 60 year old female presented with sudden painless loss of vision in right eye. She is known diabetic from last ten years. Her glycosylated Hb is 10.5mg%. On fundus examination, there is no glow in right eye while in left eye multiple dot and blot haemorrhages with hard and soft exudates in all the quadrants.

a) What is the most probable diagnosis?
b) Features of Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy
c) Screening protocol for diabetic retinopathy
d) Define Clinically Significant Macular Edema (CSME) (2x4=8)

A 70 year old male presented with progressive diminution of vision in both eye (RE>LE) from last 5 years. On examination both eye anterior segment examination was within normal limit except cataract in both eye.

a) Write stages of cataract formation
b) Difference between IMSC and MSC
c) Steps of Small Incision Cataract surgery (SICS)
d) Viscoelastics (2+2+2+1=7)

A 65 year male operated for Right eye Cataract presented after 3 days with complaints of pain, blurring, redness, photophobia and headache. On examination best corrected vision in RE was count fingers close to face with accurate PR. Slit lamp examination revealed mild to moderate lid edema, circumciliary congestion, corneal edema, signs of anterior uveitis, and absent fundal glow on retro-illumination. Based on this information answer the following-

a) What are provisional & differential diagnoses?
b) What further examination & investigations are needed?
c) Write treatment (2.5+2.5+3=8)
Write briefly: Complicated cataract

qnaData.stem:
60 years old gentleman present with painless, slowly progressive decrease in vision in his both the eyes for the past 1 year. BCVA in both eyes 6/60. Slit lamp examination revealed cortical and nuclear cataract with amber colour and IOP- 16 mm Hg in both the eyes.

a) Clinical diagnosis with nuclear grade and classification system
b) Further clinical and laboratory investigations
c) Enumerate different surgical options available and preferable surgical technique for the patient with merits and demerits (2+2+3=7) [7]

52 years old lady presents with sudden onset painful swelling below root of nose near inner corner of the left eye for the past 5 days with watering. Ocular examination reveals tender, cystic, inflamed swelling at left lacrimal sac area with regurgitation of purulent fluid on pressing lacrimal sac area left side.

a) Most probable clinical diagnosis and differential diagnosis
b) Complications

A 42 year old man presented to Ophthalmology OPD with complaints of pain, redness, blurring of vision and photophobia in Left eye for past 10 days. He is a farmer by occupation and has been working in the rice fields for past 15 days. On examination, whitish lesion is seen over Cornea which stains positively with Fluorescein stain and whitish content is observed in the anterior chamber inferiorly. Anterior chamber depth is normal and pupils are normal in size, shape and reaction.

i) Enumerate the differential diagnosis for this case.
ii) What is the most probable provisional diagnosis for this case?
iii) Describe the clinical features of the provisional diagnosis.
iv) Discuss the management of this case. [1+1+3+3=8]

A 62 year old female presented to ophthalmology OPD with complaints of pain, redness and diminution of vision in LE for the last 24 hours along with episodes of vomiting. Her vision was 6/6 and HM respectively in RE and LE respectively. She had history of intermittent pain and heaviness along with colour haloes in both her eyes for last 2-3 months. On examination IOP in 14 mm and 42 mm of Hg in both eyes respectively along with shallow anterior chamber. Left eye pupil was mid dilated and non reacting to light.

A) What is the most probable provisional diagnosis.
B) Describe the stages, clinical features of the provisional diagnosis.
C) Outline steps of its management plan with the help of a line diagram. [1+4+2=7]

A 30 Yrs old male, watchman by profession presents with sudden painless diminution of vision for four days. He does not have diabetes or hypertension for but was on topical steroid ointment for psoriasis for last two years. His vision is 6/18 in RE and 6/6 in LE. Anterior segment examination is normal bilaterally. Fundus examination of RE reveals neurosensory detachment at the macula. Fundus fluorescein angiography shows ink blot leak in parafoveal area temporally.

a. What is the most probable diagnosis?
b. Discuss the pathophysiology and risk factors of the disease.
c. Enumerate two characteristic appearance on fluorescein angiography
d. Discuss the management options and What will be the initial management in the aforesaid situation? [1+2+2+2=7]

A 3 year old baby was brought to the OPD by his mother with complaints of constant watering from his both eyes along with color changes of his cornea. On examination there was intense light sensitiveness & blepharospasm. The eyeball was prominent. The cornea was large and bluish in appearance.

a. What may be the most probable diagnosis and it's differentials?
b. How will you approach this child and the tests will you do to reach the diagnosis?
c. Describe the classical corneal signs in this case.
d. Enumerate the expected pathogenesis behind this case.
e. Describe the different surgical approaches in this case including contraindications of specific anti-glaucoma medications. [1+2+1.5+1.5+2=8]

A 48 year old female presented to Ophthalmology OPD with complaints of severe pain, redness, marked blurring of vision and photophobia in Right Eye (RE) since 1 day which started when she was weaving sweater late in the evening. Visual acuity is 3/60 in RE and 6/6 in Left Eye (LE), IOP is 58 mm of Hg in RE and 16 mm of Hg in LE. Cornea was hazy in RE. Anterior chamber depth is shallow in both eyes. Pupil is mid-dilated, non-reacting in RE. No significant abnormality was noted in systemic examination. Based on this information:

i) Enumerate two differential diagnosis of sudden painful blurring of vision
ii) What is the most probable provisional diagnosis for this case

A 45 year old male presented to the Ophthalmology OPD with chief complaints of watering in Left eye (LE) for past 2 years. It gets aggravated on exposure to wind. There is regurgitation on applying pressure over lacrimal sac area in LE. You decide to perform a lacrimal sac syringing procedure.

i) Discuss how you can diagnose the level of lacrimal passage obstruction by simply performing the lacrimal syringing test
ii) In the above case, if you observe regurgitation through upper punctum on performing syringing through lower punctum in LE, what is your most likely diagnosis? How is the site of obstruction in this case different from a congenital case of nasolacrimal duct obstruction?
iii) Enumerate the surgical options for this case

qnaData.stem:
A one year old child is referred from Paediatrics OPD to Ophthalmology OPD for complaint of watering from both eyes for past 1 month, which gets aggravated on exposure to light. There is no discharge or stickiness of eyes. Mother also reports that the child’s eyes appear large and bulging and that the child avoids exposure to light and gets irritated with it. options: Both the focal lines are behind the retina
Both the focal lines are in front of reina
One focal line is on the retina while the other is behind it
Both the focal lines are on the retina

Assertion: Cycloplegic drug Homatropine is used for refraction in children
Reason: Homatropine is used to uncover mainly the facultative component of hyperopia
options: A (Assertion TRUE, Reason TRUE, R explains A)
B (Assertion TRUE, Reason TRUE, R does NOT explain A)

qnaData.stem:
Assertion: Topical steroid drops are indicated in cases with severe alkali eye burns with total corneal epithelial defect
Reason: Topical steroids enhance corneal collagen synthesis which helps prevent corneal collagen breakdown
options: A (Assertion TRUE, Reason TRUE, R explains A)
B (Assertion TRUE, Reason TRUE, R does NOT explain A)

qnaData.stem:

Reason: Corticosteroids through oral route alone can be associated with increased risk of recurrence of the disease

B (Assertion TRUE, Reason TRUE, R does NOT explain A)

qnaData.stem: A 35 yr old female patient presents with swelling and pain over the left lacrimal sac area for 3 days. She has past h/o of watering with discharge from that eye. What will be the first line of management.
options: Medical management with antibiotics and antiinflammatory
qnaData.chapter: 47. Lacrimal apparatus : Anatomy, Watering eye and Dry eye

qnaData.stem: 32 years gentleman with myopia and occasional contact lens wearer, an avid swimmer present with sudden onset redness and extreme pain in his left eye for the last 5 days. Slit lamp examination showed ring shaped infiltrates in corneal stroma with epithelial defect. All are true about the patient disease except:
options: Immediate corneal scraping with smear and blood agar culture to be done
Confocal microscopy is a good non-invasive way to diagnose the condition
Polyhexamethylene biguanidine (PHMB) 0.02% or Chlorhexidine 0.02% are effective treatments of such cases
Viral keratitis and fungal keratitis are the main differential diagnoses of this condition

qnaData.stem: 35 years old gentleman present sudden onset redness and pain in his right eye for the past 3 days. Slit lamp examination reveals miotic pupil, cells and flare in anterior chamber with relatively fixed hypopyon. He has past history of low backache. Following are true except:
options: Immediate oral steroids should be started
Topical Prednisolone along with Homatropine should be started
X ray lumbar spine should be done to rule spondylitis
VDRL/TPHA should be done to rule out syphilis

qnaData.stem: 72 years old gentleman was referred for cataract surgery. He has asymmetric poor pupillary light reactions with good pupillary reaction to near stimuli. Dilatation with tropicamide was poor. Most likely condition:
qnaData.chapter: 7. Pupillary Reflexes, Light Reflex and its Lesions

qnaData.stem: 40 years lady presented with headache with loss of peripheral vision along with weight gain and abnormal nipple discharge. Visual field and MRI findings are as shown below. The most likely diagnosis is:
qnaData.chapter: 6. Visual Pathway and Visual Field Defects

qnaData.stem: A 75-year-old man with a history of cataracts presents to the emergency department with severe eye pain, redness, and vision loss in his right eye. He reports that his vision has been deteriorating over the past few weeks. On examination, his right eye shows significant conjunctival injection, a hazy cornea, and a mid-dilated pupil. The intraocular pressure (IOP) is measured at 50 mmHg. The patient has a white cataract that appears to be leaking a milky substance. What is the most likely underlying mechanism causing his glaucoma?
options: Accumulation of inflammatory cells in the anterior chamber
Lens material entering the anterior chamber and obstructing aqueous outflow

qnaData.stem: A 55 year old patient complains of white frothy secretions on lid margin. On examination, there was plugging of the meibomian gland opening, vertical yellowish streaks shinning through conjunctiva and hyperemia of posterior lid margin. Most likely diagnosis is
Following is TRUE for Abducens nerve palsy:
options: Eye ball will be deviated outwards
Eye ball will be deviated upwards
Eye ball will be deviated inwards
Eye ball will be deviated downwards
